questions.explanation:
1.
A square has area 16 cm2. What is its perimeter in centimetres?
(A) 4
(B) 8
(C) 12
(D) 16
(E) 24
The square has side length 4 cm therefore perimeter 16 cm,
hence (D).

2.
Marlie was born in 2016. In which year will she turn 14?
(A) 2026
(B) 2028
(C) 2030
(D) 2032
(E) 2034
Since 2016 + 14 = 2030, she will turn 14 in 2030,
hence (C).

3.
At a theme park, one ride costs $8. How many rides can Sally buy with her $65?
(A) 5
(B) 6
(C) 7
(D) 8
(E) 9
The closest multiple of 8 is 8 × 8 = 64, so Sally can buy 8 rides,
hence (D).

4.
Evan set the temperature dial on his oven as shown.
What temperature is it set to?
(A) 155 ◦C
(C) 180 ◦C
(B) 170 ◦C
(D) 220 ◦C
(E) 230 ◦C
OFF
100
150
200
250
◦C
Between 150 and 200 there are 5 intervals, so each interval represents 10 ◦C. The dial is
set 3 intervals above 150 ◦C which is therefore 180 ◦C,
hence (C).

5.
The following decimals are to be placed in increasing order.
0.42 0.24 4.02 2.4 0.042
Which number will be in the middle position?
(A) 0.42
(B) 0.24
(C) 4.02
(D) 2.4
(E) 0.042
When we put the 5 numbers in ascending order, they are 0.042, 0.24, 0.42, 2.4, 4.02, and
so the middle number is 0.42,
hence (A).

7.
Leila has found out three facts about the speeds of her four favourite fast birds:
• The spur-winged goose (SWG) is slower than the white-throated needletail (WTN).
• The peregrine falcon (PF) is the fastest bird.
• The frigate bird (FB) is faster than only one of the other birds.
Which of the following lists gives the birds from fastest to slowest?
(A) SWG, WTN, PF, FB
(C) PF, WTN, SWG, FB
(B) PF, SWG, FB, WTN
(D) FB, PF, WTN, SWG
(E) PF, WTN, FB, SWG
Using the abbreviations in the question, place them in order as required. PF is fastest, so
must be first. FB is the second slowest, hence third. The other two birds must be 2nd
and 4th, but SWG is slower than WTN.
So fastest to slowest is PF, WTN, FB, SWG,
hence (E).

8.

school day, but part of it has been hidden.
The hottest part of the day is at 2 pm. What does
the missing section of the graph look like?

The temperature rises from 1pm to 2pm but then falls. Picture (D) is the only image
showing a rise and then a fall,
hence (D).

9.
In Primeland, they use coins worth $2, $3 and $5.
Which of the following totals is not possible using exactly 3 coins?
(A) $6
(B) $8
(C) $10
(D) $12
(E) $14
Possible combinations of 3 coins are
2 + 2 + 2 = 6
2 + 2 + 3 = 7
2 + 2 + 5 = 9
2 + 3 + 3 = 8
2 + 3 + 5 = 10
2 + 5 + 5 = 12
3 + 3 + 3 = 9
3 + 3 + 5 = 11
3 + 5 + 5 = 13
5 + 5 + 5 = 15
This includes options (A), (B), (C) and (D), but not (E),
hence (E).

10. The cube pictured has six different faces.
Which of the following is not a possible picture of this
cube?
×
□
⃝

×

⃝

×
Pictures (D) and (E) are possible by rotating the diagram of the cube by 120◦
anticlockwise and clockwise, respectively. Picture (A) shows the same faces, but the
order of the red-blue-green faces is clockwise in the question and anticlockwise in (A). So
(A) is not possible. The other two options are possible: (B) by having the red face
downwards and (C) by having the green face downwards. Consequently the only picture
that is not possible is (A),
hence (A).
Questions 11 to 20, 4 marks each
11-20 题，每题4 分

11. The Balga is a grass tree found in south-western Australia.
Balgas grow between 1 and 2 centimetres taller each year.
Approximately how old is a Balga that is 4 metres tall?
(A) 10 years
(B) 30 years
(C) 60 years
(D) 300 years
(E) 1000 years
Each 100 years, a Balga will grow between 1 and 2 metres. To reach 4 metres will take
between 4 centuries, at 1 metre per century, or 2 centuries, at 2 metres per century. Of
the answers given, 300 years is the best approximation,
hence (D).

12. What fraction of this rectangle is shaded?
(A) 1
2
(B) 5
12
(C) 1
3
(D) 2
5
(E) 5
9
The rectangle has 10 congruent equilateral triangles, and 4 smaller triangles, each being
one half the area of the equilateral triangles. So the rectangle is equal to 12 of the
equilateral triangles. Since 5 of these are shaded, the fraction shaded is 5
12,
hence (B).

(D) A gift card and a roll of wrapping paper
(E) A magazine and a roll of wrapping paper
Since Roberta paid $16, she spent more than $8 on the most expensive item, which is
either a book or a gift card. If her expensive item was a $10 book, then she spent $6 on
her other item at half price. But then her cheaper item would have cost $12, which does
not work. So her expensive item can’t have been a book.
If her expensive item was a $12 gift card, she spent $4 on the other item, which must
have been priced at $8, which is the wrapping paper. So Roberta bought a gift card and
a roll of wrapping paper,
hence (D).

14. A rectangular block measures 4 × 3 × 2 in centimetres. All of its
surfaces are painted and then it is cut into 24 cubes of side
length 1 centimetre.
How many cubes have exactly one face painted?
(A) 4
(B) 8
(C) 12
(D) 18
(E) 24
In the top layer, only the centre two cubes have a single painted face. Similarly in the
bottom layer, the centre two cubes have a single painted face. Consequently, 4 cubes
have exactly one painted face,
hence (A).

15. James convinces his parents to start a new plan for paying him pocket money. In week 1,
they will give him 10 cents and every week after that, they will double the amount they
pay him.
In which week will he first receive more than $10 pocket money?
(A) 6
(B) 7
(C) 8
(D) 9
(E) 10
Tabulate the amount received each week:

From this table, week 8 is the first week with more than $10,
hence (C).

16. This graph shows the percentages of different
eye colours of the students in Year 6.
In Year 6, 48 students have brown eyes.
What is the total number of students in Year 6?
0%
10%
20%
30%
40%
Brown
Blue
Green
Black
(A) 96
(B) 100
(C) 120
(D) 132
(E) 144
From the graph, 48 students are 40% of the students. So 48 ÷ 4 = 12 students is 10% of
the students and 120 students is 100% of the students,
hence (C).

50 cm

(A) 2
(B) 4
(C) 6
(D) 8
(E) 10
Tiles can be placed horizontally
or vertically
One possibility is to have 5 vertical tiles:
Otherwise the space above or below any horizontal tile must also have horizontal tiles,
making a triple of horizontal tiles:
The remaining two tiles must be vertical, and there are 3 ways to arrange these: (i)
(ii)
(iii)
So there are 4 different ways to tile the rectangle,
hence (B).

18. I meant to add two numbers together and then subtract a third number.
Unfortunately I got my plus and minus symbols the wrong way round. But I was lucky!
The answer still came out to be correct.
Which statement must be true?
(A) The first number equals the sum of the other two.
(B) The third number is the difference between the other two.
(C) The first and second numbers are equal.
(D) The second and third numbers are equal.
(E) The third number is zero.
The difference between the two calculations is that the second and third numbers both
change from add to subtract, or vice-versa.
So if the second and third numbers are the same, the answers will both be equal to the
first number. If the second number is bigger than the third, then the first calculation
would be bigger than the first number and the second calculation would be smaller than
the first number, so the two answers will not be the same.
If the second number is smaller than the third, the two answers will not be the same, for
similar reasons.
So the only way to have the two answers the same is for the second and third numbers to
be the same. So (D) must be true.
To see that (D) is the only statement that is must be true, a simple example like
5 + 2 −2 = 5 = 5 −2 + 2 is one where (D) is true but (A), (B), (C) and (E) are all false,
hence (D).

19. A square piece of paper is folded along the dotted lines, as shown in the diagrams. The
bottom-right corner is then cut off, as shown by the shading in the third diagram.

When the paper is unfolded, which of the following is the result?
(A)
(B)
(C)
(D)
(E)
Work backwards from the cut shape:
The shape is clearly (A),
hence (A).

20. The number 15 is divisible by its unit digit, that is, 15 ÷ 5 = 3.
How many whole numbers between 20 and 50 have this property?
(A) 12
(B) 14
(C) 16
(D) 18
(E) 20
Consider such a number, like 25. Subtract its units digit to make a second number. This
number has a units digit equal to 0: 25 −5 = 20. Also 20 is a multiple of 5, the units
digit.
In general this second number will be a multiple of 10, being one of 20, 30 or 40, and also
a multiple of the unit digit.
Going back to the units digit, this will be a factor of this second number. So we make a
table of possibilities
Multiple of 10
Possible units digit
Count
20
1, 2, 4, 5
4
30
1, 2, 3, 5, 6
5
40
1, 2, 4, 5, 8
5
This gives 14 numbers: 21, 22, 24, 25, 31, 32, 33, 35, 36, 41, 42, 44, 45 and 48,
hence (B).
Questions 21 to 25, 5 marks each
21-25 题，每题5 分

21. Troy makes a list of 6 numbers by writing down a starting number then writing the next 5
counting numbers, going up in steps of 1. When he adds them, the total is less than 100.
He increases each number by 1, and the new total is greater than 100.
What is this new total?
(A) 101
(B) 102
(C) 103
(D) 104
(E) 105
Consider the smallest case: 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6 = 21. The next case will be
2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 7 = 27 since all 6 numbers have been increased by 1. The next will be
27 + 6 = 33, then 33 + 6 = 39 and so on. So when you add six consecutive numbers
together, you will always get 21 plus a multiple of 6.
Nearest to 100 are 21 + 78 = 99 and 21 + 84 = 105, which can be found to be
14 + 15 + 16 + 17 + 18 + 19 = 99 and 15 + 16 + 17 + 18 + 19 + 20 = 105,
hence (E).

22. To prepare a garden bed for growing ginger, Xiao makes a special mix of ginger soil. She
first mixes 2 buckets of manure with 4 buckets of compost. Then she mixes 1 bucket of
this mixture with 3 buckets of sand to make ginger soil.
How much compost is in each bucket of ginger soil?
(A) 1
6 of a bucket
(B) 1
8 of a bucket
(C) 2
9 of a bucket
(D) 1
10 of a bucket
(E) 1
12 of a bucket
Alternative 1
After the first mixing, Xiao has 6 buckets. If she then used this to make 6 batches of
ginger soil, she would use 18 buckets of sand to make 24 buckets of ginger soil.
These 24 buckets would contain 4 buckets of compost, so that the soil is one-sixth
compost. Then each bucket of soil contains 1
6 of a bucket of compost,
hence (A).
Alternative 2
The mixture of manure and compost is 1
3 manure and 2
3 compost. So the final 4 buckets
of soil contains 2
3 of a bucket of compost. One bucket of this soil is 1
4 of these final 4
buckets, so it contains 2
3 × 1
4 = 2
12 = 1
6 of a bucket of compost,
hence (A).

23. A deck of playing cards originally contained 52 cards. Vicki loses some of these. When
she deals the cards she has left equally between four people, she has three cards left over.
When she deals them equally between six people, she has one card left over. Which of
the following could be the number of cards Vicki lost?
(A) 2
(B) 10
(C) 11
(D) 17
(E) 21
Alternative 1
For each option, consider how many cards would be left. Option A corresponds to 50
cards left. Option B corresponds to 42 cards left. Option C corresponds to 41 cards left.
Option D corresponds to 35 cards left. Option E corresponds to 31 cards left. We
consider each of these.
When the remaining cards are divided by 4, the remainder is 3. Since 50 ÷ 4 = 12
remainder 2, option A is eliminated. Since 42 ÷ 4 = 10 remainder 2, option B is
eliminated. Since 41 ÷ 4 = 10 remainder 1, option C is eliminated. Since 35 ÷ 4 = 8
remainder 3, option D is a possible answer. Since 31 ÷ 4 = 7 remainder 3, option E is a
possible answer.
When the remaining cards are divided by 6, Vicki has one left over. Since 35 ÷ 6 = 5
remainder 5, option D is eliminated. This leaves 31 ÷ 6 = 5 remainder 1, so option E is
the only correct answer,
hence (E).
Alternative 2
The number of cards left is 1 more than a multiple of 6 and 3 more than a multiple of 4.
Examining multiples of 6, the possibilities that are also 3 more than a multiple of 4 are 7,
19, 31 and 43. This corresponds to losing 45, 33, 21 and 9 cards. Of these, only 21 is
given,
hence (E).

24. The area of this rectangle is 56 square centimetres.
The midpoints of BC and DC are joined to A and to
each other, to form the shaded triangle shown. What
is the area of this triangle in square centimetres?
D
A
C
B
(A) 14
(B) 21
(C) 24
(D) 25
(E) 28
Join the midpoints of all 4 sides to divide the ABCD into 4 smaller congruent rectangles,
each of area 56 ÷ 4 = 14 cm2:
D
A
C
B
P
Q
R
S
O
Now consider the areas of the 3 white triangles. Rectangle APRD has area 28 cm2 so
triangle ARD is half this, or 14 cm2. Similarly, triangle ABQ has area 14 cm2. Rectangle
OQCR has area 14 cm2, so triangle QCR is half this, or 14 ÷ 2 = 7 cm2.
Then triangle AQR has area 56 −14 −14 −7 = 21 cm2,
hence (B).

25. Kiko and 5 of her friends are seated around a
restaurant table.
Each person introduces themself, telling
everyone how many of the people at the table
they’ve previously met. Naturally, Kiko has
met all 5.
Coincidentally, the other 5 have all met the same number as each other.
Which of the following is not a possibility for this number that all 5 of Kiko’s friends
have met?
Jason
Kiko
Joanne
Michael
Cameron
Bessie
(A) 1
(B) 3
(C) 4
(D) 5
(E) All of (A) to (D) are possible.
On the diagram, join two people with a line if they know each other. Each line has two
ends, so the total number of ends for all lines is even. Kiko has 5 ends, and all 5 of her
friends have the same number of ends.
If the 5 friends have an even number of ends each, they have an even number between
them, and adding Kiko’s 5 ends makes an odd total, which is not possible. On the other
hand, if they each have an odd number of ends, they have an odd number, and adding
Kiko’s 5 makes an even number, which is possible.
This means that 4 is not a possible number of ends on each of the 5 friends. The odd
possibilities 1, 3 and 5 are all possible, as shown below.
Ja
K
Jo
M
C
B
5 + 5 × 1 = 10 ends
Ja
K
Jo
M
C
B
5 + 5 × 3 = 20 ends
Ja
K
Jo
M
C
B
5 + 5 × 5 = 30 ends
So (C) is the only number that is not possible,
hence (C).
For questions 26 to 30, shade the answer as a whole number from 0 to 999
in the space provided on the answer sheet.
问题26-30 的答案为0-999 之间的整数，请将答案填涂在答题卡对应区域。
Questions 26–30 are worth 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10 marks, respectively.
第26-30 题分别为6, 7, 8, 9, 10 分。

26. Jane has four doting grandparents who each separately tell her that they will help her
with her savings plans.
• Granny Ava says, ‘Whatever you have when you come to me, I will add $10.’
• Granny Beth says, ‘Whatever you have when you come to me, I will double it.’
• Grampa Charles says, ‘Whatever you have when you come to me, I will halve your
savings but then add $50.’
• Grampa Dave says, ‘Whatever you have when you come to me, I will double it and
add $5.’
Jane has saved $10 by herself. She plans to visit each grandparent once, in the order that
maximises the amount she has at the end. In dollars, what is this maximum amount?
No matter the order of the visits, the $10 Jane starts with is doubled twice and halved
once, becoming $20. The other amounts added are $5, $10 and $50. These end up being
worth more if they are followed by doubling steps, and no halving steps. In particular,
visiting C first means that only the original $10 is halved, and the $50 added will later be
quadrupled. After visiting C, Jane has $55.
The second visit is best to be A so that the $10 is quadrupled later. Jane then has $65.
The last two visits each double this $65, but it is better to visit D then B so that the $5
is doubled once. In this order, Jane has $135, then $270,
hence (270).

27. The numbers 1 to 6 are arranged to form two 3-digit numbers that are then added
together. What is the difference between the largest and smallest sums that can be
obtained?
The largest sum which can be formed is 642 + 531 = 1173, by using the largest digits in
the larger place values. (Other examples with the same largest sum are possible, such as
632 + 541 = 1173.) The smallest is 135 + 246 = 381. The difference between them is
1173 −381 = 792,
hence (792).

28. Skip counting by 5, I write a list of 400 numbers that starts 5, 10, 15 and ends 1990,
1995, 2000.
How many times do I write the digit ‘0’?
Every second multiple of 5 ends in 0. This gives 200 zeros in the units place.
Among the three- and four-digit numbers, there is a zero in the tens place for the
numbers 100, 105, 200, 205, . . . , 1900, 1905 and 2000. This adds 19 × 2 + 1 = 39 zeros.
Finally, there are extra zeros in the hundreds place for the numbers 1000, 1005, 1010,
1015, . . . , 1090, 1095 and 2000. This gives another 2 × 10 + 1 = 21 zeros.
Therefore there are 200 + 39 + 21 = 260 zeros in total,
hence (260).

29. Starting with the number 2024, a second number is calculated by squaring each digit and
adding these squares together: 22 + 02 + 22 + 42 = 24. The third number is then
calculated by adding the squares of the digits of the second number. This continues until
the 999th number. What is the 999th number?
The first few numbers are 2024, 24, 20, 4, 16, 37, 58, 89, 145, 42, 20, . . .. So the 3rd number
is the same as the 11th number. After this the sequence will cycle through these 8
numbers. Since 1000 is a multiple of 8, the 1000th number will be the same as the 8th
number. So the 999th number will be the same as the 7th number, which is 58,
hence (58).

30. To cross a river, there are 13 stepping stones in a line, equally spaced 50 centimetres
apart.
I would like to start at the first and finish at the last, and step on each stepping stone
exactly once. I can take both 50 centimetre and 1 metre steps, both forwards and
backwards.
How many ways are there for me to cross the river?
We work through the cases for the number of stepping stones, until we find a pattern and
a rule.
Stones
Possibilities
Count
2
1
3
1
4
2
5
3
6
4
7
6
In this table, the possibilities are grouped by whether they start with a 50 cm step or a
100 cm step. If I start with a 50 cm step, the number of ways to continue is the same as
the number of ways to cross with one stone fewer. If I start with a 100 cm step, the next
two steps must be 50 cm back then 100 cm forward, and then the number of ways to
continue is the same as the number of ways to cross with three stones fewer.
This tells us the pattern in these numbers: 1 + 3 = 4, 2 + 4 = 6 and the next one will be
3 + 6 = 9. We continue this pattern:
Stones

60
So there are 60 ways to cross the river like this,
hence (60).
